description: Curated, advisory ingredient substitution reference for fond. Context-tagged (baking / sauteing / general), ranked, and sourced. Ratios are culinary facts; substitutions are advisory only and never auto-applied to a recipe. See THIRD_PARTY.md and docs/due-diligence/substitution-dataset.md.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup buttermilk = 1 cup milk + 1 tbsp lemon juice; stir and rest 5-10 min
substitutions.caveat: In baking, the acidity reacts with baking soda for lift — use an acid-based swap, not plain milk, or the crumb won't rise the same.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup buttermilk = 1 cup milk + 1 tbsp white vinegar; stir and rest 5-10 min
substitutions.caveat: Slightly sharper than lemon; the same acid-plus-soda leavening applies in baking.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup buttermilk = 3/4 cup plain yogurt + 1/4 cup milk or water
substitutions.caveat: Thicker and tangier; thin to a pourable consistency and expect a denser crumb.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup buttermilk = 3/4 cup sour cream + 1/4 cup milk or water
substitutions.caveat: Adds richness and tang; best where extra fat is welcome.

substitutions.ratio: 1 tbsp butter ≈ 1 tbsp oil (or scale ~3/4 by volume for a leaner result)
substitutions.caveat: No browning or milk-solid flavor, but a higher smoke point — good for sauteing, not for flavor-forward finishing.

substitutions.ratio: 1:1 by weight or volume
substitutions.caveat: Use stick (not soft/spreadable, which is higher in water) or texture and spread suffer; flavor is flatter.

substitutions.ratio: 1:1 by volume
substitutions.caveat: Solid at room temperature so it creams passably, but melts differently; refined avoids coconut flavor. Structure and spread will shift.

substitutions.ratio: 1 egg = 1 tbsp ground flaxseed + 3 tbsp water; rest 5-10 min until gel
substitutions.caveat: Works as a binder, not for structure or lift — good in quick breads/cookies, poor in cakes that rely on eggs for rise.

substitutions.ratio: 1 egg = 1/4 cup applesauce
substitutions.caveat: Adds moisture and a little sweetness while reducing rise; best in muffins and quick breads, not delicate cakes.

substitutions.ratio: 1 egg = 1/4 cup mashed banana (about 1/2 banana)
substitutions.caveat: Binds and adds moisture but carries banana flavor; suits sweet quick breads.

substitutions.ratio: Follow package directions per egg
substitutions.caveat: Formulated for binding and some lift; results vary by brand.

substitutions.ratio: 1 tsp baking powder = 1/4 tsp baking soda + 1/2 tsp cream of tartar
substitutions.caveat: Single-acting — mix into the batter and bake right away; it won't hold a second rise.

substitutions.ratio: Use about 3 tsp baking powder per 1 tsp baking soda
substitutions.caveat: Baking powder is weaker and already contains acid, so it can throw off the recipe's salt and acid balance — results are approximate.
Very close in acidity and moisture for baking; when cooking, stir in off-heat to avoid curdling.
Richer and less tangy, and more stable to heat.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup sour cream = 1 cup heavy cream + 1 tbsp lemon juice; rest to thicken
substitutions.caveat: Thinner and milder; works as a pourable tangy cream, not a scoopable one.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup heavy cream = 3/4 cup whole milk + 1/4 cup melted butter
substitutions.caveat: Adds richness for cooking and baking but will NOT whip — don't use it where whipped volume is required.
Good body for sauces and soups; won't whip and is slightly cooked in flavor.
Lower fat — sauces thicken less and it can break at a hard boil; won't whip.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup brown sugar = 1 cup white sugar + 1 tbsp molasses (2 tbsp for dark)
substitutions.caveat: Restores the moisture and caramel notes brown sugar provides; whisk well to distribute.
Loses moisture and molasses flavor — baked goods turn out drier and lighter; last resort.

substitutions.ratio: Use 2 tbsp flour per 1 tbsp cornstarch
substitutions.caveat: Thickens cloudier and less glossy; cook longer to lose the raw-flour taste.

substitutions.ratio: 1:1 (or slightly less)
substitutions.caveat: Clear and glossy and good for acidic sauces, but thins if boiled hard or held long — add near the end.

substitutions.ratio: Use about 2 tsp per 1 tbsp cornstarch
substitutions.caveat: Glossy and strong; can turn stringy if overheated.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup self-rising flour = 1 cup all-purpose flour + 1 1/2 tsp baking powder + 1/4 tsp salt
substitutions.caveat: Whisk the leavening and salt in thoroughly; if the recipe already adds salt/leavening, don't double up.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup honey = 1 1/4 cup sugar + 1/4 cup water
substitutions.caveat: Less moisture retention and no honey aroma; in baking you may need to tweak liquid and browning.
Thinner with its own flavor; reduce other liquid slightly in baking.

substitutions.ratio: Use about 2/3 cup agave per 1 cup honey (agave is sweeter)
substitutions.caveat: Milder and thinner; adjust sweetness to taste.

substitutions.ratio: 1/2 cup white wine = 1/2 cup chicken or vegetable broth + 1 tsp white wine vinegar or lemon juice
substitutions.caveat: Non-alcoholic; adds acidity for deglazing but not the wine's depth. Add acid to taste.

substitutions.ratio: 1/2 cup white wine = 1/2 cup white grape juice + 1 tsp vinegar
substitutions.caveat: Sweeter — best in dishes that can carry a little sweetness; balance with the vinegar.

substitutions.ratio: 1/2 cup red wine = 1/2 cup beef broth + 1 tsp red wine vinegar
substitutions.caveat: Non-alcoholic; savory depth without the tannins or fruit. Add vinegar to taste.

substitutions.ratio: 1/2 cup red wine = 1/2 cup red grape juice + 1 tsp vinegar
substitutions.caveat: Sweeter; best in braises and sauces that can absorb some sweetness.
Similar tang and body with more fat; stir into hot dishes off-heat to prevent breaking.

substitutions.ratio: Strain regular yogurt 30-60 min to thicken, then 1:1
substitutions.caveat: Regular yogurt is thinner — strain it or expect a looser result.
Richer and sweeter with less tang; softer set, so frostings and cheesecakes are looser.

substitutions.ratio: 1:1 (strain until thick)
substitutions.caveat: Tangier and softer with far less fat — fine for dips and spreads, weaker for set desserts.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup milk = 1 cup water + 1 tbsp melted butter
substitutions.caveat: Replaces the liquid and a little fat, but not milk's proteins/sugars — browning and richness suffer.

substitutions.ratio: 1 cup milk = 1/2 cup evaporated milk + 1/2 cup water
substitutions.caveat: Close to whole milk once diluted; faintly cooked flavor.
Choose unsweetened for savory dishes; flavor and protein vary by type (soy behaves closest to dairy).
Similar acidity with a different aroma; fine where brightness matters more than lemon flavor.

substitutions.ratio: Use about half as much, to taste
substitutions.caveat: Sharper and without citrus aroma — good for acidity in savory cooking, less so for flavor.
